item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's something going on that means Ashley and any other possible survivors had to stay up high. Storm cellar, blocking pipes, holing up in the attic - all to get further away from ground level and whatever is down there. Safe bet they get dragged under the earth (hence the psychic quilt of taphophobia) and kept alive for a little while? That explains the all the nasty in the water samples. This is that good good spooky and I am HERE for it. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After mentioning taphophobia and getting PTSD of The Buried from the Magnus Archives i was hoping for some good ol' "buried alive" scene in the exploration log. Sadly, that expectation was my undoing as it brought upon me disappointed when the last part of the exploration log was just a corridor with a door at the end.

Tho, i would've still enjoyed the other parts — disappointed or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was first going for no-vote, but coming here and reading the explanations of the author helped me to finish connecting dots in my head. Since the explanation makes sense, I think an upvote is deserved, it really made for an enjoyable reading.

As a matter of fact I won't go for the Reality Bender thing, I find the very definition lame. This child was either a psychopath, deformed or both, and somehow something in the whole situation sparked its connection to the earth, there is no need for a previous anomaly.

The question that remains is how much did the sheriff actually knew of the whole thing. It can be anything from just noticing something going on and avoiding it, all the way up to participating in the original mess and fearing consequences.

item.title: Re: Not clear or long enough
item.content:encoded: Yeah, if I have to read this much author's explanation to understand what is going on instead of gleaning sufficient information from the actual article itself, then it doesn't work for me either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the best depictions of D-class I ever saw. Their dialogue is perfect. Kudos

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ha. I *literally came here just to say* that this one reminded me of the SAR series from r/nosleep, especially the bit about the vanishing storm cellar/stairs, and what do I see in the first comment but the author stating it was part of their inspiration. awesome work. now I want to go reread the SAR series all over again. it definitely scared the crap out of me when I read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I imagine prior to 1975, South Mountains State Park was all private land, but if you're a local from the NC backwoods, that really doesn't matter much. Western NC does have the reputation of landowners hiding out with shotguns to deter trespassers but that mostly applies to out-of-staters and developers if the landowner's a local you probably go to his church and he invites you onto his property to go fishin'. I think changing it to "they went camping in the area that is currently South Mountains State Park" or even something more specific, e.g. "they went on a fishing trip along the Henry Fork Catawba River" would be fine.

Using real-life locations in SCPs is great imo it makes it much easier to visualise the surroundings.

+ show block
– hide block

The long unpaved road into the park, all these crystal clear streams shrouded in impenetrable rhododendron thickets, primitive campgrounds surrounded with the sound of rushing water, hills rising in the distance, here and there scattered throughout the park the remains of abandoned houses and townsites (in some of which only the cemetery remains always a cool/creepy thing to come across at 8 pm on a summer hiking trip), and then maybe in some area off limits to visitors guarded by uniformed "park rangers" perhaps under cover of being a protected area set aside for the cerulean warbler (Setophaga cerulea) or something the remains of a child that was buried alive filter up through the earth. So I do think it adds something that a [REDACTED] wouldn't, but maybe again that's just because I know the place….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Belated response (I only just saw this post now!):

I really ought to make up fictitious locations more often. I usually use real-life ones because it forces me to do research (which prevents me from making silly mistakes), but I can do that by just basing my fictitious locations off a real-life location (see my recent article: SCP-4182).

Re: SMSP's establishment, while that's true, nothing's stopping anyone from going camping in the 50s in a forest that isn't a park! It's way more dangerous (no one's going to notice if you've gone missing), but I imagine more experienced campers might have given it a try (and even taken their families if they were feeling particularly arrogant).

I might be completely wrong about this, though. In which case — I'm mildly tempted to change the park to use a fictional name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mild nightmare retardant due to the setting—I spent a lot of my childhood at South Mountains State Park and had lots of fun there :p not your fault though.

I'm not sure if this is intentional or not, but (in real life) SMSP wasn't established until 1975, whereas the story mentions characters going on a camping trip there in the 1950s. With the timeline you have the Foundation containing this anomaly in 1972, so I guess you could work around it by claiming that the park was established at the request of the Foundation to protect the area from development or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations! You succesfelly received my upvote and 100+!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something that I particularly liked about this was the characterization of the D-Class personnel in the exploration logs: the way the first one saw which way the wind was blowing and just noped out, and also the banter between the newbie and the vet in the second log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love this skip! Really great job setting up the story! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This skip does a great job with atmosphere and setting, which has been stated before, but I was so glad that you linked to the SRA story as I could not stop thinking of that as I was reading :)
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly like how instead of the creepy thing just staying underground, it literally infests the underground, which is a much more “reality bender” thing to do in my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the best reads I've had in a while! I've actually read it three times because it's just… so good.

I definitely get the series 1 vibes, and all the little details really set this skip apart. (the taphophobia was what hooked me 100%)

Definitely my new favourite SCP, +1 !

item.title: Re: Not clear or long enough
item.content:encoded:
I mean, definitely don't be dissuaded by this from writing your own story. I think what's going on here is so deeply tucked away that you could easily get away with retelling it in a different format.

I never really intended for anyone to "get" the full story anyway; I mostly just wanted readers to bake their own version of the story from similar ingredients (something something "the basement has turned the ground sour and it's rising up to get us" something something).

item.title: Re: Not clear or long enough
item.content:encoded: Oh shit. D: I've had a half-finished article draft for forever that basically has this exact story premise, except it's nowhere near as good. ;_; Well at least somebody got it right.

item.title: Re: Not clear or long enough
item.content:encoded:
"Being hunted" scares? I'm not sure what you're talking about.

I've explained this a couple of times in other posts in this very thread, but I'll break it down one more time here — this time, thoroughly:

Breakdown
– hide block

The first thing we hit is the title: "This Sour Earth". Okay. This is a skip about earth that's gone sour. That's your first clue.

#2: The containment procedures make it clear the Foundation isn't fucking with this thing. If you end up getting lost for longer than an hour just in the exclusion zone, they're not going to bother trying to retrieve you. If they think someone's gone missing, they just burn the evidence. Okay, whatever this thing is, it must be fucked up and too dangerous for the Foundation to fuck with. That's your second clue.

#3: The description mentions its properties. Shifting topography, animals don't want to go in there, humans ('sapient organisms') experience topographical agnosia (loss of your sense of direction), electromagnetic interference (makes sending drones in tricky, and can fuck with electric devices like phones and radios — important for later), audio anomalies… a lot of different weird properties. The important one is that it causes personnel to experience taphophobia, which is a 'fear of being buried alive'. I wanted the reader to walk away from the description with that in their head — that's why I put it last. That's your third clue. "Being buried alive".

#4: The first incident report provides us with a lot of clues, all of which start fleshing out the story:

  • One of the children is scratched out in the photograph. Why? Because someone didn't want us to know about this kid. They wanted this kid 'erased'; forgotten.
  • The family dog was maimed by some sort of 'unidentified animal'.
  • There are scratching sounds under the floorboards (IE, in the basement).
  • No one either looked in the basement or reported looking in the basement.
  • The Cotter family plastered all their pipes shut (in most houses, pipes come in through the basement and go up into the house) and stayed in the attic. They barricaded themselves up there.
  • The house was reportedly demolished, but no evidence corroborates this claim.

That's a lot of information, but here's what it amounts to: There's something in the basement. The Cotters are hiding from it in their attic. It's rising up from the pipes. The police don't want to deal with it.

The title is "Sour Earth", and presumably this is what's souring it. It has something to do with that child they scratched out of the picture, probably. Also, fear of being buried alive — fear of being trapped inside your basement. Forever.

The third incident mentions a set of doors out in the wilderness, with stairs leading down. I never use the words "storm cellar", but that's precisely what I'm describing. Remember the rancid smell in the Cotter House? It's here, too. This is the Cotter House's storm cellar. This is the basement. Again: Something is in the basement. Something that wants to get out. It wants you to go down there. It wants you to let it out.

The incident with the Bonafedes doesn't seem super-relevant at first, beyond emphasizing that the cops know something they're not telling anyone (because they pull the search away from the SCP). But then during the exploration log we find their camp ("A B" — Ashley Bonafede — also the baby bottle, there's a baby in the photo), and it comes up that someone (the Bonafedes) were hanging out in the trees for a while — living up there. Why? Because the earth is sour. Because there's something in the ground trying to rise up — and as it does, it pulls people down.

The last incident, with the water — this is just escalation. Showing that the thing under the ground is trying to get out, rising up through the pipes (again). It's decaying organic matter — again, this implies that the Cotter family did something to their child. Something in the basement. Something that didn't quite "Take". And now that child is trying to "escape".

The logs hammer this home one more time. Both logs feature characters tripping over things that are popping up from the ground (a pipe in the first log, and a cement tile in the second). Again, something's rising up, trying to get out, trying to escape. In the final log, we have D-ALPHA hiding in the trees, just like the Bonafedes (that chocolate bar that looks like it's from the "1950s" is supposed to be another clue that this is the Bonafede camp, btw). D-ALPHA hears Radio telling him to come back, but after following the directions, he starts to realize it's just repeating everything the radio previously said. It's not actually the Foundation's command — it's just something pretending to be, trying to get him out of the tree, back on the ground. So, he runs.

And when he runs, he finds — a house. A two story house (just like the Cotter's) with a light on in the attic (where did the Cotters hide, again? In the attic. They're still up there, still hiding from the thing in the basement. The thing that won't let them leave. The thing that wants to get out). He decides to go down into the basement, trying to escape from the sounds outside… but the sounds outside are just lures, things the thing in the basement produces to get people to go down there with it. Because if it can't get out, it'll bring everything down there with it.

And that's how it ends: The door opens. We don't get to see what's actually in the basement, because whatever it is, it's too fucked up for us to actually see it.

The fact that the camera is found later on the edge of the anomaly — that's purely just ex deus machina. I allow myself one per article — one thing that probably shouldn't have happened, but had to happen for the story to work. I had to explain how the camera got back to the Foundation, so I just said "fuck it, they found it on the edge of the anomaly".

TL;DR: This post (by multizig) basically got it right. SCP-3219 is the Cotter's daughter. She's a reality bender. Her parents ended up trying to kill her and bury her body in the basement (probably after she fucked up the dog). But it didn't quite work. She actually wasn't dead (reality benders are weird).

People in the town noticed she was 'missing', but also knew she was fucked up and had weird magic powers so they didn't want to talk about it. They tried to just erase her. But she wouldn't let them. Her attempts to be remembered — to get out of the basement — are what's made this earth gone sour.

I mean, she's been stuck down there for nearly a century, now. She's a little "sour" about it (ha ha ha). Wouldn't you be?

SIDENOTE: This is all author head-canon. Feel free to devise your own story. My explanation is genuinely no more valid than yours. But yes, an actual explanation for what going on existed in my head, and — outside of the camera being recovered — it fits the information presented.

item.title: Not clear or long enough
item.content:encoded:
I like SCPs that have unsolved mysteries, but here, it feels like nothing coherent is going on. There's something dangerous about water, and something dangerous is out there, but we don't get enough information about it to have the slightest clue. I could see this as one of the more magiscientific scips where the deal is a really low Hume level and not a specific anomaly, but that's not what the article implies. It doesn't give us a silhouette of a monster, it only gives us people reacting to the silhouette of a monster. If we had seen what was on the other side of the door when it opened, or known what was in the storm cellar, or if there had been a couple more exploration logs (since the Foundation definitely would conduct more explorations until they at least got the shape of what they were dealing with) this would be an amazing SCP. But as it stands, it's just incomplete. -1 unless you can explain to me what it is and how to figure that out from the article.

Also, how the heck do the "being hunted" scares tie in with the supernatural reshaping of the environment? The whole stairs appearing from nowhere and topography reshaping itself just feel unrelated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh; a few other small things I wanted to note: the calm, dead repetition of the radio voice in the second log is terrifying and almost Ligottian. A+ job.

And I was so happy for D-311366 noping out and refusing to be the 1-in-100 D-classer that we usually see in exploration logs! Nice subversion there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the ending would work better if it were a little more ambiguous, but other than that, I really like this. There's an abstract quality where it feels like all these unsettling details fit together into something horrific, but you never really lead the readers directly there. I think a lot of the best SCPs have that quality, and I'm always happy to encounter it. The story I'm imagining is probably pretty far from what you had in mind, but I'm enjoying the disorienting dream logic that's bubbling up in my brain now.

This was pretty eerie late night reading. +1

item.title: Description of grammatical changes
item.content:encoded:
Describing the grammatical changes here, so as to not confuse moderation.

ninety kilometer > ninety-kilometer

When writing a number and a unit of measurement in full, they should be hyphenated.

storm-cellar > storm cellar

"Storm" is not an adverb or adjective, so there should not be a hyphen here. Furthermore, a different instance of this as it was written didn't contain a hyphen anyway.

Sutherland, North Carolina… > Sutherland, North Carolina, …

When writing a location that includes a state, if there's anything else more specific (in this case, a city), a comma always goes after the state.

CO (Commanding Officer). > commanding officer.

This abbreviation is not used anywhere else in the article, so it is grammatically incorrect to use it here. Generally you only use an abbreviation like this if it's used 3 times or more.

The fuck. > The fuck?

This is a question, not a statement or command.

…tripped on: A partially buried… > …tripped on: a partially buried…

It's rare that the first word after a colon is capitalized, but if it is, then the part of the sentence after that colon must itself be complete sentence in its own right. Even then, I've found sources saying it's never correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I once tried to make 3219. I failed. You did much better than me. Good job. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s like a delicious mix of the Blair Witch Project and a vintage horror novel. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got a mix of Blair Witch Project and I'm a Search and Rescue Officer vibes here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is some good classic spooky. The description and incident reports didn't a whole lot for me, but the two exploration logs were very fun and creepy.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spooky.
Very, very spooky.
+1.

My headcanon as to what's going on
Someone cared?

So, one of the children of Cotter family was probably a reality-bender, or something like that. As they discovered their abilities, they probably caused a significant chaos, which resulted in their imprisonment in the basement.

Afterwards, they either expired or continued to cause chaos, and that's where we are today, with the entire area now being an anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry you feel that way! I'm pretty comfortable with the piece as it stands; that being said, I'm definitely interested in any opinions on how the ending could be improved. I wasn't sure how else to close it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was going fine up until the last log which felt so underwhelming and generic.
Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, that's a lot of good information. Even if it's not applicable to everyone, its still good to see how others come up with their ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't want to lead you astray; my style of writing is deeply improvisational, which makes it hard for me to nail down precisely why what I do works when it does. That being said, sure — but take everything I'm saying here with prescription grade salt:

Tips? I guess?
– hide block
  • For something like this, you don't actually need to know what's going on. But you do have to have a vague idea of what's going on. For example, I know the Cotter family had a secret; it had something to do with one of their children and their basement. I know they plugged their drains and faucets up because something was rising up through the pipes from below. I know that's also why they hid in the attic. I also know that's why the Bonafedes hid in the trees.
  • Once you have your 'vague idea', the trick is to get the reader to understand what's going on without ever telling them what's going on. If you just tell the reader: "There's a monster in the basement, and it's bleeding out into the soil! It's coming up through the pipes, the concrete, even through the goddamn water!" — that's kind of scary, sure… but not half as scary if you put the reader in a place where they come to realize that without ever being told.
  • The moment the reader realizes that something is there just to be spooky, the jig is up. That's why the above tactic works so well: Once the reader gets a sense that "something is going on here", they'll start connecting those dots for you — they'll even connect dots you never intended them to connect! The flip-side of this: Don't include elements unless you have at least a vague idea of why they're there. Example: Why do animals refuse to enter SCP-3219? Answer: "They can sense that the earth there has gone sour." Why does this place cause people to develop a fear of being buried alive? Because basements are scary places that are buried underground. There doesn't have to be a concrete, clear answer, but there has to be a thematic/narrative thread. It has to make sense emotionally, if not logically.
  • To put this another way: You're more or less engaging in a game of writing prompts with the reader. Your job, then, is to make sure those writing prompts are 1) Compelling, 2) Thematically consistent, and 3) Part of a steady escalation. This way, you kind of end up collaborating with the reader to help them create their own story to fill in the gaps you left.
  • Monsters are scary, but the absence of a monster can be even scarier. In the (paraphrased) words of Stephen King: No matter how scary you make your 7-foot tall cockroach, readers will always think "Scary, but at least it wasn't an 8-foot tall cockroach!". Not showing the monster is how you leave room for the reader to terrify themselves.
  • In the (again, paraphrased) words of SoullessSingularity: Death isn't scary. Death is a release. Death is when the horror ends. But not knowing what happened? That's way, way scarier.
  • ETA: Something else that's terrifying (which is something that I relied on heavily for this piece) — behavior that makes no sense to us, but leaves us with the sense that it made perfect sense to the people who did it. The act of plugging your faucets and drains up with plaster — hiding in your attic — hiding in the trees. Why would people do that? We don't know, but if we believe they had good reasons, we'll find ourselves afraid of finding out what those good reasons were.

I kind of wrote this all off the cuff, because I'm on my way out the door. Hopefully some of it helps? Either way, good luck!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love scips like this. I've always wanted to write one, but I'm never sure I could make it interesting enough, or maintain a good internal consistency. Any tips?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! And no, that's a grammatical error (that I repeated xD). Fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a rhyme and reason, here. A few notable details that might help:

Spoilers!
– hide block
  • One of the people in the Cotter family picture has their face 'scratched out'. There's a reason.
  • The investigators mention a lot of things in their search of the Cotter house; the backyard, the sounds under the floorboards, the signs of extended habitation in the attic, the rancid smell… but there's one thing they don't mention. One part of the house we're told exists, but about which, no details are provided. ETA: I changed this to make it explicitly pointed out in the article (as I think it's a detail that was otherwise way too easy to miss).
  • Why did the Cotter family feel the need to 'plug up' all their faucets and drains with plaster?
  • The incident report mentions the house was demolished, but no evidence exists corroborating this.
  • During the search for the Bonafede family, two rescuers disappeared — and rather than keep looking, the sheriff moved the search elsewhere. The sheriff knows something.
  • D-BETA finds the initials 'A B' on the tree branch — who's initials are those? (Hint: One of the pictures tells you!)
  • It's mentioned that the house D-ALPHA comes across has a set of lights on in the attic.
  • Why was someone (or multiple someones) 'living in the trees' and not in the camp-site?
  • The doors to a set of stairs that the park ranger found in the woods? That was a storm cellar.

(Not to imply that there's some deep, secret truth going on; just that a lot of the things going on in this skip are done for specific reasons, and meant to get you thinking about very specific possibilities)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, we need a good old unexplained anomaly with super-spooky exploration logs every once in a while. :)

Bonafede family

This messed me up because I kept thinking of the Boniface family. <.<

Also, I was going to change "as much as the tent", but it shows up twice so I guess that's intentional? Anyway, good stuff, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty sure I have no damn clue what's going on here, but that last log is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good. Properly spooky.

+1

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
the bitter taste, the hidden face
of the lost forgotten child
the darkest need
the slowest speed
the debt unreconciled

CREDIT
– hide block

PICTURES:

  • https://pxhere.com/en/photo/1389425
  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:StateLibQld_1_149791_Family_of_George_and_Ailsa_Green,_ca._1940.jpg
  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sylvan_Scene_with_Boat_and_Hanging_Clothes_-_Hippopotamus_Lake_-_Near_Satiri_-_Burkina_Faso.jpg
  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:John_og_Lina_Sandvik_med_familie_(24850278741).jpg
  • https://www.flickr.com/photos/139701443@N04/25812649910
  • https://pixabay.com/en/tunnel-industrial-shelter-asphalt-2801762/

INSPIRATION:

  • psul's SCP-3515.
  • Search and Rescue Woods

FEEDBACK:

  • minmin, who suggested some (after-posting) changes to highlight what's actually going on (as I was perhaps just a bit too cagey about it).

Not sure if this will work; we'll see! It's something of an experiment — a throwback to Series 1. I wanted to try something that was just straight-up old-school spooky without relying on anything that was shocking, gross, or obvious. Basically, I was aiming to make something that's scary mostly on account of its atmosphere and the story it implies, rather than the story it tells.

If it doesn't seem to catch on, I'll probably take it down and maybe try a more extensive rewrite at some later point.

Interested in reading more of my nonsense? Check out my author page.
